column2: In a whole slide image of focal proliferative IgA nephropathy, you would observe scattered, hypercellular areas within the glomeruli, often appearing as small, round or ovoid lesions. These areas are characterized by an increased number of mesangial cells and matrix, with a prominent thickening of the capillary walls. Immunohistochemical staining for IgA would show intense deposition along the mesangial areas and occasionally along the capillary walls, appearing as granular or linear patterns. The surrounding glomerular architecture may appear normal or mildly affected, with occasional mild interstitial fibrosis and mild tubulointerstitial inflammation. Capillary loops within these lesions may be dilated or occluded, contributing to the overall appearance of hypercellularity and mesangial sclerosis.

In a whole slide image of focal proliferative IgA nephropathy, you would observe characteristic features such as segmental or focal thickening of the glomerular capillary walls, often accompanied by mesangial hypercellularity and expansion. There is typically an accumulation of immunoglobulin A (IgA) deposits within the mesangium, which may appear as granular or linear on immunofluorescence. The basement membrane may show splitting or C3 deposition, and there can be associated interstitial fibrosis and mild tubulointerstitial changes. Capillary loops may appear dilated and tortuous, and in some cases, there might be areas of crescent formation or hyalinosis. These findings are often distributed in a focal or segmental pattern across the glomeruli.

In a whole slide image of focal proliferative IgA nephropathy, you would observe scattered, hypercellular areas within the glomeruli that appear as small, round to oval nodules, often with a pale, eosinophilic appearance due to the deposition of IgA immune complexes. These lesions are typically not diffuse but rather patchy and can be surrounded by a zone of sclerosis or fibrosis. The basement membranes may appear thickened and irregular, and there might be associated mesangial cell proliferation and expansion of the mesangial matrix. Additionally, there could be mild to moderate interstitial fibrosis and occasional tubulointerstitial inflammation, which may appear as scattered inflammatory cells or as more extensive infiltration depending on the severity of the disease. Capillary loops within these lesions may show partial or complete occlusion, contributing to the characteristic histological features of this condition.

In a whole slide image of focal proliferative IgA nephropathy, you would observe scattered, hypercellular areas within the glomeruli that appear as small, round to oval nodules, often with a pale, eosinophilic appearance due to the deposition of IgA immune complexes. These lesions are typically not diffuse but rather patchy and can be surrounded by a zone of sclerosis or fibrosis. The basement membranes may show thickening and splitting, and there might be associated interstitial inflammatory cell infiltration and mild tubulointerstitial changes. Capillary loops within these lesions often exhibit dilatation and may have a "double contour" appearance due to the thickened basement membrane and endothelial cell swelling. Additionally, there may be occasional hemorrhage or necrosis within the affected glomeruli, contributing to the overall heterogeneous and patchy pattern observed in the image.

In a whole slide image of focal proliferative IgA nephropathy, you would observe characteristic features such as segmental or focal thickening of the glomerular capillary walls, often accompanied by mesangial hypercellularity and expansion. There is typically an accumulation of immunoglobulin A (IgA) deposits within the mesangium, which may appear as granular or linear on immunofluorescence. The basement membrane may show splitting or C3 deposition, and there can be associated interstitial fibrosis and mild tubular atrophy. Capillary loops may appear dilated and tortuous, and the overlying endothelium may be irregular or hyperplastic. These changes are usually more pronounced in one part of the glomerulus than in others, hence the term "focal."

In a whole slide image of focal proliferative IgA nephropathy, you would observe scattered, hypercellular areas within the glomeruli, often appearing as small, round or nodular lesions. Renal tubules may exhibit slight atrophy or hypertrophy, and there might be mild interstitial fibrosis and inflammatory cell infiltration, usually consisting of lymphocytes and macrophages.
